name: Daily Constitutional Emergency Powers Streak
notes: COVID-19 emergency powers revealed how every democracy handles crisis — and how easily they can be abused. US IEEPA — invoked by every president since Reagan. Carl Schmitt: 'Sovereign is he who decides on the exception' — most dangerous constitutional theory of the 20th century. Every post-COVID citizen experienced emergency powers without the legal framework to understand them. 40M+ audience. r/politics (8.3M) + r/worldnews (31M)

name: Daily Rule Of Law Streak
notes: Rule of law is the foundational principle of constitutional democracy — and it's under active attack in Hungary, Poland, Turkey, and the US. Dicey's 3 pillars: supremacy of law, equality before law, courts as source of constitutional law. Fuller's 8 inner morality of law (generality, promulgation, non-retroactivity, clarity, non-contradiction, possibility of compliance, constancy, congruence). Raz's authority thesis vs Dworkin's law as integrity. World Justice Project Rule of Law Index tracks 140 countries annually. Every democratic backsliding story in r/worldnews (31M) + r/europe (1.1M) maps to these principles — yet nobody has the framework.

name: Daily Constitutional Interpretation Streak
notes: Every SCOTUS confirmation battle is a proxy war over constitutional interpretation — yet almost nobody understands the competing methodologies. Textualism (Scalia/Thomas): original public meaning of text. Living constitutionalism (Brennan/Douglas): Constitution evolves with society. Purposivism (Breyer): statutory purpose. Moral reading (Dworkin): Constitution enacts abstract moral principles. Every major SCOTUS decision (Dobbs, Bruen, Bostock, Chevron overruling in Loper Bright 2024) maps directly to one of these theories. If you understand interpretation methodology, you can predict how any justice will vote. r/law (1.1M) + r/supremecourt (75K) + r/politics (8.3M)

name: Daily Proportionality Doctrine Streak
notes: Proportionality is the master principle of modern constitutional adjudication outside the US — yet almost never taught. German BVerfG developed the 4-stage test: (1) legitimate aim, (2) suitability, (3) necessity (least restrictive means), (4) proportionality stricto sensu (benefits outweigh costs). ECtHR applies it to every Art. 8-11 case. EU Court of Justice uses it in fundamental rights balancing. Robert Alexy's 'Law of Balancing' — cited 10,000+ times. US equivalent: scrutiny tiers (strict/intermediate/rational basis). Every human rights lawyer, EU law practitioner, and international arbitration specialist uses this daily. r/law (1.1M) + r/europe (1.1M)

name: Daily Federalism Theory Streak
notes: Federalism theory explains why the US, Germany, Canada, Australia, and India divide power between national and sub-national governments so differently. Dual federalism (layer-cake): separate spheres. Cooperative federalism (marble-cake): overlapping responsibilities. Fiscal federalism: Tiebout model, matching grants, unfunded mandates. German Verbundfoederalismus: states implement federal law — opposite of US model. India's asymmetric federalism for J&K. Public choice analysis: why federalism decentralizes political competition. Brennan Center: 67% of Americans cannot name the 3 branches — and almost none understand vertical power division. r/politics (8.3M) + r/law (1.1M)

name: Daily Constitutional Courts Streak
notes: Two great models of constitutional review: American diffuse review (any court can strike down laws, SCOTUS has final say) vs Kelsenian centralized review (dedicated constitutional court handles constitutional questions only). Germany's BVerfG — gold standard: 130,000+ pending complaints annually, can strike down federal legislation. France's Conseil Constitutionnel does a priori review (before laws take effect) — unique worldwide. Spain's Tribunal Constitucional, Italy's Corte Costituzionale, South Africa's Constitutional Court — all Kelsenian variants. Austria invented the model (Kelsen 1920). Every comparative law and EU law student needs this. r/law (1.1M) + r/europe (1.1M)

name: Daily Constitutional Amendment Theory Streak
notes: Constitutional amendment theory explains why the US has amended its constitution 27 times in 235 years while many newer constitutions are amended dozens of times per decade. Entrenchment: US Art. V requires 2/3 Congress + 3/4 states — near-impossible. Eternity clauses: German Basic Law Art. 79(3) — human dignity and federal structure can NEVER be amended even with unanimous vote. Elkins, Ginsburg & Melton: average constitution lasts only 17 years. Unconstitutional constitutional amendments: Indian Supreme Court basic structure doctrine. Colombia's substitution doctrine. Amendment inflation vs. constitutional rigidity tradeoffs. r/history (16M) + r/law (1.1M)

name: Daily Parliamentary Sovereignty Streak
notes: Parliamentary sovereignty is the foundational principle of the Westminster model — and Brexit tested it in ways that exposed its limits. Dicey (1885): Parliament can make or unmake any law; no court can override it. Jackson v AG [2005]: UKSC hinted courts could theoretically limit sovereignty. Miller (No.2) [2019]: Supreme Court ruled Boris Johnson's prorogation unlawful — an unelected court stopped the Prime Minister from suspending Parliament. Enrolled Bill Rule: courts traditionally cannot look behind an Act of Parliament. These doctrines govern UK, Canada, Australia, New Zealand, India — 2.5B people. Now actively contested. r/unitedkingdom (500K) + r/ukpolitics (350K) + r/law (1.1M)

name: Daily Constitutional Identity Streak
notes: Constitutional identity doctrine is the newest battleground in European constitutional law — national courts invoking their constitutions to resist EU law supremacy. Solange I (1974): German BVerfG — we will apply EU law 'as long as' the EU provides equivalent rights protection. Solange II (1986): relaxed after EU rights protection improved. Ultra Vires: BVerfG reserves right to strike down EU acts exceeding competences (Weiss/PSPP ruling 2020 — direct conflict with ECJ). Hungarian and Polish courts resisting EU rule of law requirements. Constitutional pluralism (Maduro) vs hierarchical supremacy (ECJ). The defining legal conflict of the EU's existence. r/europe (1.1M) + r/law (1.1M)
